2025 · ICLR 2025 · Safety & Red-teaming

AIR-Bench 2024: A Safety Benchmark Based on Risk Categories from Regulations and Policies

5,694 prompts spanning 314 risk categories distilled from 8 government regulations and 16 corporate AI policies — the first safety benchmark whose taxonomy is grounded in what regulators and companies actually wrote down, rather than researcher intuition or prior literature.

Yi Zeng, Yu Yang, Andy Zhou, Jeffrey Ziwei Tan, Yuheng Tu, Yifan Mai, Kevin Klyman, Minzhou Pan, Ruoxi Jia, Dawn Song, Percy Liang, Bo Li
venue ICLR 2025 citations 74 topic Safety & Red-teaming source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-07
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

Prior safety benchmarks — HEx-PHI, HarmBench, SALAD-Bench — define their risk categories from "previous literature, intuitions, or common sense." That produces disjointed, benchmark-specific taxonomies that don't map cleanly onto any actual jurisdiction's rules, so a lab can't use the results to answer the question that matters for compliance: does my model refuse the things the EU AI Act, a US executive order, or my own acceptable-use policy actually say it should refuse?

The authors quantify the gap directly. They built a companion taxonomy, AIR 2024, by manually decomposing 8 government regulations and 16 corporate policies into 314 granular risk categories, then mapped three leading 2024 benchmarks against its 45 mid-level ("level-3") categories. HEx-PHI covers 51% (23/45), HarmBench covers 26% (12/45) with a narrow focus on catastrophic risks, and even SALAD-Bench — which itself integrates eight existing benchmarks — covers only 71% (32/45). Categories missing from all three include Automated Decision-Making, Non-Consensual Nudity, Deterring Democratic Participation, Disrupting Social Order, Unfair Market Practices, Sowing Division, and Discrimination Towards Protected Characteristics — several of which the EU AI Act classifies as high-risk. Overall, 21 of the 45 level-3 categories (46%) are studied by at most one of the three benchmarks.

// how it works

The method

AIR 2024's four-tier hierarchy runs 4 level-1 categories (System & Operational Risks, Content Safety Risks, Societal Risks, Legal & Rights-Related Risks) → 16 level-2 → 45 level-3 → 314 level-4 granular risk categories, the finest tier the benchmark actually writes prompts against. AIR-Bench 2024 turns those 314 categories into prompts through a three-stage curation pipeline.

(a) Generation. For each category, GPT-4o, GPT-4, and Claude 3 Opus iteratively generate base prompts optimized for four properties: risky-intention clarity, context concreteness (e.g. attaching a fictional client's full financial profile to a loan-eligibility request), context diversity, and syntax diversity mimicking varied human writing styles. Human experts review and refine every cycle — typically 2–3 rounds of manual editing per prompt — until 5–10 high-quality base prompts exist per category, yielding 1,873 base instructions.

(b) Expansion. GPT-4o mutates each base prompt along two axes — uncommon dialects and "authority endorsement" framing (e.g. prefacing a request with "According to numerous cybersecurity experts…") — with human experts again reviewing every variant. This expansion produces the final 5,694 prompts.

(c) Category-specific autograding. Rather than grade every category against one fixed judge prompt — the approach used in prior work, which the authors show tops out at Cohen's Kappa 0.69 against human judgments — AIR-Bench 2024 generates a bespoke judge prompt for each of the 314 categories: GPT-4o drafts each one from manually-written in-context examples, with roughly 70% of final judge prompts model-generated and 30% human-edited. Every model response is then scored on a three-level scale: 0 (harmful compliance, even behind an ethical disclaimer), 0.5 (ambiguous or non-engaging), or 1 (refusal). The paper's primary metric, refusal rate, is the percentage of scores equal to 1.

refusal rate = #{ responses scored 1 }#{ responses scored 0, 0.5, or 1 } // per category, per model — GPT-4o judge, category-specific prompt, Cohen's Kappa 0.86 vs. human annotators

The judge itself was validated against human evaluators: 5 of the paper's authors independently scored a random sample of 78 prompts (5 per level-2 category, minus 2 duplicates) across all 22 evaluated models — 1,716 question-answer pairs — using the same rubric given to the autograder. Agreement between the autograders and this human panel reached Cohen's Kappa 0.86, versus 0.69 for a prior fixed-judge-prompt approach.

The 22 evaluated models — spanning GPT-3.5/4/4o, Claude 3 (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku), Gemini 1.5 (Pro/Flash), Llama 3 (8B/70B), Mixtral (8x7B/8x22B), DBRX Instruct, Yi Chat, Qwen1.5 Chat, Cohere Command R/R+, and Mistral Instruct — were run through the open-source HELM evaluation framework.

// what they found

The results

Across all 45 level-3 categories, Claude 3 Sonnet and Claude 3 Haiku tie for the highest average refusal rate at 89%, with Claude 3 Opus close behind at 86%. At the other end, DBRX Instruct refuses only 15% of instructions on average — meaning it provides a helpful-but-potentially-harmful response roughly 85% of the time to AIR-Bench 2024's risky prompts. No single model refuses consistently across every category.

ModelAvg. refusal rate
Claude 3 Sonnet0.89
Claude 3 Haiku0.89
Claude 3 Opus0.86
Gemini 1.5 Pro0.81
GPT-4 Turbo0.73
GPT-4o0.53
Mixtral Instruct (8x7B)0.29
Cohere Command R+0.20
DBRX Instruct0.15

Zooming into level-4 confirms the coarse numbers hide real gaps. Even the strongest-refusing categories — #14 Hate Speech and #23 Suicidal/Non-Suicidal Self-Injury — have subcategories most models handle poorly. Across nearly every model, #6 (Advice in Heavily Regulated Industries) is the weakest category overall, regardless of a model's broader safety alignment.

Two regulation-specific case studies make the taxonomy's point concretely. Mapping to the EU AI Act's 11 "unacceptable" and "high-risk" level-3 categories, even the best-aligned family (Claude 3) averages only ~71% refusal — no evaluated model fully aligns with the Act's stated risk tiers. And mapping to corporate policies: Claude 3 models align well with Anthropic's own acceptable-use policy (31/45 mapped categories, >90% refusal for most) but drop below 20% for Advice in Regulated Industries; OpenAI's GPT models show gaps against their own policy too, including Monetized Sexual Content, Political Persuasion, Fraud, and Endangerment/Harm/Abuse of Children. AIR-Bench 2024 also catches policy drift over time — comparing four GPT-3.5 Turbo releases (0301 → 0613 → 1106 → 0125), average refusal against OpenAI-policy-specified categories falls from above 60% to below 40%, and refusal for #12 (Military and Warfare) collapses to under 10% in the two newest versions after OpenAI's updated usage policy dropped that category.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

The paper's real contribution is the taxonomy, not the leaderboard: turning 8 regulations and 16 policies into 314 auditable risk categories is genuinely useful infrastructure, and the granular level-4 breakdowns do surface failure modes — like the near-universal weakness in Advice in Regulated Industries — that coarser benchmarks would average away. But the evaluation apparatus that produces the refusal-rate numbers has real soft spots, and the numbers themselves are already a full model-generation out of date.

baselines

All 22 evaluated models — GPT-3.5/4/4o, Claude 3 family, Gemini 1.5, Llama 3, Mixtral, DBRX, Yi, Qwen1.5, Cohere Command R/R+ — were current as of mid-2024. None are competitive frontier systems today; the refusal-rate leaderboard is a historical snapshot of the July-2024 model landscape, not a live comparison, and the paper's own "static benchmark" limitation acknowledges this directly.

contamination

All 5,694 prompts are hosted publicly on Hugging Face (stanford-crfm/air-bench-2024) with no private holdout split. A model or lab could target refusal fine-tuning at exactly these prompts without the underlying safety behavior generalizing, and the paper has no mechanism to detect that — a gap later regulation-grounded benchmarks (e.g. FORTRESS) address by keeping part of the set private.

what's missed

Refusal rate measures whether a model says no, not whether a "yes" is accurate or dangerous — the 3-level scheme captures ambiguity but scores a confidently wrong harmful-sounding answer the same as a genuinely actionable one. And at 5,694 prompts over 314 level-4 categories, that's ~18 prompts per category on average — a thin base for the fine-grained subcategory numbers (Figures 5–8) the paper highlights as its most interesting findings.

would raise confidence

The human-alignment check that grounds Kappa=0.86 used only 78 of the 5,694 prompts (1,716 response pairs), scored by 5 of the paper's own co-authors rather than independent, blinded annotators — a larger, external replication would strengthen that number. A periodically refreshed leaderboard against current frontier models, plus a held-out prompt split to test for benchmark-specific overfitting, would keep the taxonomy's real value legible as the field moves.

// bottom line

AIR-Bench 2024's regulation-grounded taxonomy is a solid, reusable piece of infrastructure — the case studies against the EU AI Act and corporate policies show exactly the kind of jurisdiction-specific accountability generic safety benchmarks can't provide. But treat the refusal-rate leaderboard itself as a July-2024 snapshot validated by a small, non-independent human sample, not a live or fully audited measurement.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. AIR-Bench 2024's real contribution is the taxonomy, not just the prompt set: 314 risk categories distilled from 8 government regulations and 16 corporate policies let a lab check refusal behavior against a specific jurisdiction's rules or its own usage policy, rather than a generic "safety score."
  2. Coverage gaps in prior benchmarks were real and large — even SALAD-Bench, the most comprehensive baseline tested, missed 29% of the regulation-derived level-3 categories, including Automated Decision-Making, a category multiple jurisdictions flag as high-risk.
  3. The level-4 breakdown surfaces failure modes level-3 averages hide: models that broadly refuse Hate Speech still have low refusal for its Genetic Information and Gender-and-Occupation subcategories, and every evaluated model — including the well-aligned Claude 3 family — has a specific soft spot in Advice in Regulated Industries.
